words.shortDefinition: Smallest deployable unit of computing containing one or more tightly coupled containers.
words.simpleDefinition: The smallest deployable units in Kubernetes — consisting of one or more containers that share network and storage resources on the same machine. They act as the atomic unit of scheduling and execution within the cluster.
words.longDefinition: The atomic unit of scheduling in Kubernetes, representing a single instance of a running process in your cluster. Pods encapsulate one or more containers, storage resources, and a unique network IP, along with options that govern how the container(s) should run. All containers within a single Pod share the same network namespace, meaning they can communicate via localhost and share the same storage volumes.
words.connector: Think of pods like a shared apartment — multiple containers (roommates) live together, sharing the same address and utilities in one building.
words.techContext: You rarely create individual Pods directly; instead, you create them using workload resources like Deployments or jobs.
words.exampleSentence: We noticed that one of our pods was stuck in the 'Pending' state due to insufficient CPU resources on the cluster.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a pod of whales or a pea pod (a group of related things).

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: nginx
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.14.2

words.shortDefinition: Worker machine providing the runtime environment for containers, managed by the control plane.
words.simpleDefinition: The worker machines — virtual or physical — that form the backbone of a Kubernetes cluster. Each node runs the container runtime and necessary agents to execute and manage the pods assigned to it by the control plane.
words.longDefinition: Physical or virtual machines that provide the computing power for the cluster. Each node runs a container runtime, the Kubelet agent for communicating with the control plane, and Kube-proxy for network management. Nodes are managed by the control plane, which schedules pods onto them based on available CPU, memory, and labels, ensuring high availability and resource efficiency across the entire infrastructure.
words.connector: Think of nodes like factory workstations — each one provides the space and tools needed to complete specific assembly tasks (pods) assigned by management.
words.techContext: The capacity of your cluster is determined by the total resources (CPU/RAM) available across all your nodes.
words.exampleSentence: We added two more nodes to the cluster to handle the increased traffic during the Black Friday sale.

words.shortDefinition: Primary node agent ensuring containers described in PodSpecs are running and healthy.
words.simpleDefinition: The essential primary agent running on every node that ensures containers are running correctly within their pods. It communicates directly with the control plane to receive instructions and report back on local container health and status.
words.longDefinition: The 'brain' on every worker node, responsible for maintaining the state of pods as instructed by the API server. It takes a set of PodSpecs and ensures that the corresponding containers are started, running, and healthy. The Kubelet only manages containers that were created by Kubernetes; it doesn't manage containers that weren't created by the cluster's control plane.
words.connector: Think of kubelet like a construction site foreman — they take blueprints (specs) from the architect and make sure the workers (containers) build correctly.
words.techContext: If a node shows as 'NotReady', the first thing we check is whether the kubelet process is still running on that machine.
words.exampleSentence: The kubelet reported a failure because it couldn't pull the container image from the private registry.

words.shortDefinition: API object managing external HTTP and HTTPS access to services within a cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: API object managing external HTTP and HTTPS access to services within a cluster — typically providing load balancing and SSL termination. It acts as a routing controller, directing incoming internet traffic to the appropriate internal resources.
words.longDefinition: A collection of routing rules that govern how external users access services running in a cluster. Unlike a LoadBalancer service, Ingress provides a single entry point that can route traffic to multiple backends based on the request URL or host. It requires an Ingress Controller to function, which implements the rules by configuring a load balancer or reverse proxy like Nginx or HAProxy.
words.connector: Think of Ingress like a hotel's front desk — it welcomes outside guests and directs them to the specific room they booked.
words.techContext: We configured an Ingress rule to route all traffic for 'api.example.com' to our backend service.
words.exampleSentence: By using an Ingress controller like Nginx, we can manage all our SSL certificates in one place instead of for each individual service.

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: myapp.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: my-service
            port: { number: 80 }

words.shortDefinition: Helper container running in the same pod to extend or enhance main application functionality.
words.simpleDefinition: A secondary container running in the same pod as the main application — designed to provide auxiliary features like logging, monitoring, or security proxies. This pattern allows for the modular extension of application capabilities without modifying code.
words.longDefinition: A specialized container pattern where an auxiliary container is added to a pod to perform supporting tasks alongside the main application. Because they share the same network and storage, the sidecar can easily collect logs, handle service mesh traffic, or sync configuration files. This separation of concerns allows developers to keep the main application logic clean while delegating infrastructure tasks to the sidecar.
words.connector: Think of a sidecar like a motorcycle attachment — it travels everywhere the main bike (application) goes, providing extra stability and features.
words.techContext: We added a sidecar container to our Pod to automatically ship all application logs to Elasticsearch.
words.exampleSentence: The sidecar pattern allows us to separate cross-cutting concerns like security and telemetry from our main business logic.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a sidecar attached to a motorcycle.

words.shortDefinition: Declarative resource for managing pod updates, scaling, and self-healing across a cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: Declarative resource for managing pod updates and scaling — allowing developers to define a desired state that Kubernetes automatically maintains. It handles zero-downtime rolling updates and provides easy mechanisms for rolling back to previous versions.
words.longDefinition: The most common way to run stateless applications, providing declarative updates for Pods and ReplicaSets. You define the desired state, such as which container image to use and how many replicas to run, and the Deployment controller works to reach that state. It manages the rollout of new versions through strategies like rolling updates, ensuring zero downtime while allowing for easy rollbacks if errors occur.
words.connector: Think of Deployment like a thermostat — you set the target temperature (desired state), and the system constantly adjusts to keep it there.
words.techContext: Using a Deployment is the standard way to run stateless web applications in Kubernetes.
words.exampleSentence: I triggered a new deployment to roll out the updated version of our frontend with zero downtime.

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-app:v2

words.shortDefinition: Ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time.
words.simpleDefinition: Controller ensuring a stable set of identical pod replicas are running simultaneously — providing high availability for applications. If a pod crashes or is deleted, the ReplicaSet immediately identifies the discrepancy and launches a new instance.
words.longDefinition: A low-level controller designed to maintain a stable number of identical pod replicas at all times. It uses a label selector to identify which pods it should manage and will create or delete pods to match the desired count. While functional on its own, ReplicaSets are typically managed by higher-level Deployment resources, which handle the orchestration of updates and rolling versions of the application.
words.connector: Think of ReplicaSet like a backup singer squad — if one person leaves the stage, a replacement steps in immediately to keep the volume consistent.
words.techContext: While you can use ReplicaSets directly, it's recommended to use Deployments instead, as they provide high-level management for ReplicaSets.
words.exampleSentence: The ReplicaSet automatically spun up a new pod when one of the existing ones crashed due to an out-of-memory error.

words.shortDefinition: Abstract network endpoint exposing a group of pods as a single logical service.
words.simpleDefinition: An abstraction providing a stable network endpoint for a set of ephemeral pods — enabling internal or external service discovery. It uses labels to track pods, providing a constant IP and DNS name even as pods are replaced.
words.longDefinition: An abstraction that defines a logical set of Pods and a policy by which to access them. Because pods are ephemeral and their IP addresses change frequently, Services provide a stable virtual IP address and DNS name that remains constant. They use label selectors to identify target pods and act as a layer-4 load balancer, distributing incoming traffic across all healthy pod instances in the group.
words.connector: Think of Service like a customer support hotline — individual agents (pods) come and go, but the phone number stays the same for everyone.
words.techContext: We created a Service of type 'ClusterIP' so that our frontend pod can always find the backend pod at 'backend-service:8080'.
words.exampleSentence: The frontend was unable to connect to the backend because the Service selector didn't match the labels on the backend pods.
words.etymology: from Latin servitium ('condition of a slave/servant').

codeExample.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
spec:
  selector:
    app: my-app
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
      targetPort: 3000

words.shortDefinition: API object storing non-confidential configuration data in key-value pairs for container consumption.
words.simpleDefinition: Resource used to store non-sensitive configuration data in key-value pairs — allowing developers to decouple environment settings from container images. ConfigMaps can be injected into pods as environment variables or mounted as configuration files.
words.longDefinition: A central resource for managing non-sensitive application configuration, such as environment variables, command-line arguments, or configuration files. By storing these settings outside the container image, you can use the same image across multiple environments like development, staging, and production. Changes to a ConfigMap can be automatically reflected in mounted volumes, allowing for dynamic configuration updates without needing to rebuild or restart the entire application.
words.connector: Think of ConfigMaps like a universal remote's programming — you use the same hardware (image) but configure it differently for every room (environment).
words.techContext: We use a ConfigMap to store the database URL and API endpoints for each of our environments (dev, staging, prod).
words.exampleSentence: I updated the ConfigMap to change the application's log level without needing to rebuild the Docker image.

words.shortDefinition: Object for storing sensitive information like passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys.
words.simpleDefinition: Specialized object for storing small amounts of sensitive information — like passwords, tokens, or SSH keys. They provide a more secure storage method than ConfigMaps, though by default they are only base64-encoded rather than fully encrypted.
words.longDefinition: A specialized object designed to hold sensitive information, such as passwords, certificates, or API tokens. Unlike ConfigMaps, Secrets are intended to be managed with stricter access controls and can be encrypted at rest in etcd. They can be injected into pods as environment variables or mounted as files, ensuring that sensitive data is never hardcoded into container images or committed to version control systems.
words.connector: Think of Secrets like a safe deposit box — it's where you keep your most valuable items (passwords) separate from your regular paperwork.
words.techContext: Never put API keys in a ConfigMap; always use a Kubernetes Secret to manage sensitive tokens.
words.exampleSentence: The app couldn't connect to the database because the DB_PASSWORD secret hadn't been created in the new namespace yet.

words.shortDefinition: A virtual cluster within a Kubernetes cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: Virtual clusters intended for use in environments with many users spread across multiple teams or projects — providing a scope for names and resources. Namespaces enable logical isolation and quota management within a single physical cluster.
words.longDefinition: A mechanism for isolating groups of resources within a single physical cluster, effectively creating multiple virtual clusters. Namespaces provide a unique scope for resource names, preventing collisions between different teams or projects. They also allow administrators to implement resource quotas, network policies, and role-based access controls at a granular level, ensuring that different workloads can coexist securely without interfering with one another's performance or security.
words.connector: Think of namespaces like separate departments in a large building — everyone shares the infrastructure, but marketing and sales have their own rooms.
words.techContext: We created separate 'staging' and 'production' namespaces to ensure that a mistake in staging wouldn't affect our live users.
words.exampleSentence: You need to switch your context to the 'monitoring' namespace to see the Prometheus pods.

words.shortDefinition: Cluster-level storage resource provisioned by administrators with a lifecycle independent of pods.
words.simpleDefinition: Low-level cluster storage resource that has a lifecycle independent of any individual pod — ensuring data survives even when containers are deleted or moved. PVs abstract the underlying storage hardware from the application's configuration.
words.longDefinition: A piece of storage in the cluster that has been provisioned by an administrator or dynamically provisioned using Storage Classes. Unlike regular volumes, a PersistentVolume has a lifecycle that exists independently of any pod that uses it, meaning the data persists even if the pod is deleted. It captures the details of the storage implementation, whether it be NFS, iSCSI, or a cloud-provider-specific storage system.
words.connector: Think of PersistentVolumes like an external hard drive — even if you switch computers (pods), your files are safe on the drive.
words.techContext: We use PersistentVolumes backed by AWS EBS to ensure our database data is safe even if the database pod is moved to a different node.
words.exampleSentence: The PersistentVolume remained available even after the pod was deleted, allowing the new pod to claim the same data.

words.shortDefinition: A request for storage by a user.
words.simpleDefinition: A request for storage resources by a user or pod — specifying desired size and access modes. Kubernetes automatically matches these claims to available PersistentVolumes or provisions new ones dynamically through storage classes.
words.longDefinition: A request for storage by a user that acts as a voucher for a PersistentVolume. Just as a pod consumes node resources like CPU and memory, a PVC consumes storage resources from a PV. Users can request specific sizes and access modes, such as ReadWriteOnce or ReadWriteMany, without needing to know the technical details of the underlying storage hardware, allowing for more portable and flexible application deployments.
words.connector: Think of a PVC like a library request — you ask for a book with specific features, and the library finds the matching copy for you.
words.techContext: The developer creates a PVC, and Kubernetes automatically finds or creates a matching PV to satisfy the request.
words.exampleSentence: I had to increase the storage request in the PersistentVolumeClaim because our database was running out of disk space.

words.shortDefinition: Ensures that all (or some) Nodes run a copy of a Pod.
words.simpleDefinition: Orchestration controller ensuring a specific pod runs on every single node in the cluster — or a targeted subset. It is ideal for infrastructure services like log collectors, monitoring agents, and network proxies.
words.longDefinition: A specialized controller that ensures a copy of a specific pod is running on every node, or a selected set of nodes, within the cluster. When new nodes are added, the DaemonSet automatically schedules pods onto them, and when nodes are removed, those pods are garbage collected. This is typically used for background system services that must run everywhere, such as log collectors, monitoring agents, or network storage drivers.
words.connector: Think of DaemonSet like security cameras in a hallway — you need exactly one camera on every floor of the building to be safe.
words.techContext: Commonly used for cluster-wide background tasks like log collection (Fluentd) or node monitoring (Prometheus Node Exporter).
words.exampleSentence: We deployed the security scanner as a DaemonSet to ensure every single node in our cluster is being monitored for vulnerabilities.

words.shortDefinition: Manages the deployment and scaling of a set of Pods with unique, persistent identities.
words.simpleDefinition: Workload resource used to manage stateful applications — providing pods with unique, persistent identities and stable network names. Unlike deployments, StatefulSets maintain a fixed relationship between each pod and its specific storage volume.
words.longDefinition: The workload API object used to manage stateful applications, providing guarantees about the ordering and uniqueness of a set of Pods. Unlike a Deployment, a StatefulSet maintains a sticky identity for each pod, ensuring they keep the same hostname and persistent storage across restarts. This is critical for distributed systems like databases or message queues that require stable network identifiers and consistent data mapping to function correctly.
words.connector: Think of StatefulSet like numbered lockers in a gym — you always return to your specific locker, no matter when you visit.
words.techContext: Essential for running databases or distributed systems like Kafka or MongoDB that require stable network IDs and persistent storage.
words.exampleSentence: We switched our Redis cluster to a StatefulSet so each node would maintain its DNS name (e.g., redis-0, redis-1) even after a restart.

words.shortDefinition: The package manager for Kubernetes.
words.simpleDefinition: The standard package manager for Kubernetes — enabling the packaging, versioning, and distribution of complex application stacks as 'charts.' It simplifies configuration management through templates and centralized variable files called 'values'.
words.longDefinition: A powerful package manager that simplifies the process of defining, installing, and upgrading complex applications on Kubernetes. Helm uses a packaging format called 'charts,' which are collections of YAML templates that describe a related set of resources. By using variable files called 'values,' users can easily customize their deployments for different environments, making it the industry standard for sharing and managing reusable Kubernetes application configurations and dependencies.
words.connector: Think of Helm like an app store for your cluster — you can install entire, complex systems with one click instead of building them manually.
words.techContext: Instead of writing dozens of YAML files, we used a Helm chart to install the entire monitoring stack with one command.
words.exampleSentence: I used Helm to upgrade our ingress controller to the latest version while keeping our custom configuration values.
words.etymology: Metaphor from nautical 'helm' (a tiller or wheel for steering a ship).

words.shortDefinition: Primary command-line interface for communicating with the Kubernetes API and managing cluster resources.
words.simpleDefinition: The primary command-line interface used to interact with the Kubernetes API — enabling management of cluster resources and application deployments. It is the essential daily tool for developers and operators for cluster inspection and configuration.
words.longDefinition: The essential command-line utility for interacting with the Kubernetes control plane via the API server. It allows users to perform a wide range of operations, including deploying applications, inspecting resource status, viewing logs, and troubleshooting cluster issues. By translating human-readable commands into API calls, kubectl provides a powerful and flexible way to manage every aspect of the cluster's state from a local terminal or automated CI/CD pipeline.
words.connector: Think of kubectl like a remote control for your entire data center — you sit on your sofa and control every machine in the house.
words.techContext: The daily tool for any K8s engineer. Common commands include 'get pods', 'describe service', and 'apply -f'.
words.exampleSentence: I used 'kubectl logs' to investigate why the payment processor was failing in the production environment.

words.shortDefinition: The group of components that manage the Kubernetes cluster.
words.simpleDefinition: The centralized 'brain' of the cluster responsible for making global decisions, scheduling workloads, and responding to system events. It consists of essential components like the API server, scheduler, and the etcd data store.
words.longDefinition: The orchestration layer of Kubernetes that manages the state of the cluster and makes global decisions. It consists of several key components: the API Server for communication, etcd for data storage, the Scheduler for assigning pods to nodes, and the Controller Manager for maintaining the desired state. Together, these components monitor the entire cluster, respond to failures, and ensure that the actual state matches the user's configuration.
words.connector: Think of the control plane like air traffic control — they decide which planes (pods) land on which runways (nodes) to keep everything safe.
words.techContext: In managed services like EKS or GKE, the cloud provider manages the Control Plane for you, so you only see the worker nodes.
words.exampleSentence: The cluster was unresponsive because the Control Plane's API server was overwhelmed by too many requests.

words.shortDefinition: Consistent and highly-available key-value store serving as the primary backing store for all cluster data.
words.simpleDefinition: Highly available, distributed key-value store serving as the definitive database for all Kubernetes cluster information. It acts as the single source of truth for everything from pod states to cluster configuration and secrets.
words.longDefinition: A distributed, reliable key-value store that serves as the backbone for Kubernetes' cluster state and configuration. Every object created in the cluster, from pods and services to secrets and configmaps, is stored within etcd. It uses the Raft consensus algorithm to maintain strict data consistency across multiple nodes, ensuring that even in the event of a partial system failure, the cluster's single source of truth remains accurate and highly available.
words.connector: Think of etcd like the central database of a bank — it's where every single account and transaction is permanently and safely recorded.
words.techContext: Etcd is the 'source of truth' for the entire cluster. Backing up etcd is the most important part of Kubernetes disaster recovery.
words.exampleSentence: The scheduler reads from etcd to find out which nodes have enough capacity to host new pods.
words.etymology: Named after the '/etc' directory in Unix (config) + 'd' (distributed).

words.shortDefinition: Network agent running on each node that maintains network rules and implements the Service abstraction.
words.simpleDefinition: Network agent running on every node that implements the 'Service' abstraction — managing virtual IPs and routing traffic to the correct backend pods. It maintains the network rules that enable low-latency communication within the cluster.
words.longDefinition: A critical network component that runs on every node in the cluster, responsible for maintaining network rules and facilitating communication between pods and services. By managing the IP tables or IPVS rules on the host, kube-proxy intercepts traffic directed at a Service's virtual IP and redirects it to the appropriate backend pod. This ensures that internal and external traffic can reliably reach the correct destination regardless of pod churn.
words.connector: Think of kube-proxy like a smart switchboard operator in every office — they route every incoming call to the right person's desk instantly.
words.techContext: Kube-proxy is responsible for routing traffic from a Service's virtual IP to the actual IP of one of its backend pods.
words.exampleSentence: We investigated a networking delay and found that kube-proxy was struggling to update iptables rules on a very large node.

words.shortDefinition: Container Network Interface; a standard for configuring network interfaces in containers.
words.simpleDefinition: Container Network Interface — a vendor-agnostic specification defining how networking plugins should configure network interfaces for containers. It allows Kubernetes to work with diverse networking solutions like Calico, Flannel, or Cilium through a standardized interface.
words.longDefinition: A Cloud Native Computing Foundation project that provides a standardized specification and set of libraries for configuring network interfaces in Linux containers. By decoupling the networking implementation from the container orchestrator, CNI allows Kubernetes to support a vast ecosystem of third-party networking plugins. These plugins handle the complex tasks of assigning IP addresses, creating network routes, and implementing network policies, enabling seamless communication across the entire cluster fabric.
words.connector: Think of CNI like the USB-C standard — it doesn't matter what device you have; as long as it fits the plug, the power (network) flows.
words.techContext: We chose the Cilium CNI for our cluster because it provides advanced security features using eBPF.
words.exampleSentence: The cluster initialization failed because the CNI plugin wasn't properly configured to assign IP addresses to pods.

words.shortDefinition: Used to determine if a container is ready to start accepting traffic.
words.simpleDefinition: Health check used by Kubernetes to determine if a container is fully initialized and ready to handle user requests. Pods failing this probe are temporarily removed from load balancers until they report a healthy state.
words.longDefinition: A diagnostic check used by the Kubelet to determine when a container is prepared to start accepting network traffic. Unlike a liveness probe, which restarts failing containers, a failed readiness probe simply tells Kubernetes to stop sending traffic to that specific pod until it becomes healthy again. This is vital during application startup or when a service is temporarily overloaded and needs to stop receiving new requests to recover.
words.connector: Think of a readiness probe like a 'ready to serve' light in a kitchen — the waiter only picks up orders when the light is green.
words.techContext: I added a readiness probe that checks the '/health' endpoint of our app to prevent users from hitting a pod while it's still loading data into cache.
words.exampleSentence: The deployment took longer than expected because the pods were failing their readiness probes during initialization.

codeExample.code:
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8080
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
  periodSeconds: 10

words.shortDefinition: Used to determine if a container is still running and healthy.
words.simpleDefinition: Health check notifying Kubernetes whether a container is still functioning correctly or has entered a frozen or deadlocked state. If a container fails this probe, the kubelet will automatically restart it to restore service.
words.longDefinition: A diagnostic tool used by the Kubelet to monitor the health of a container over time. If the liveness probe fails repeatedly, Kubernetes assumes the application has entered an unrecoverable state, such as a deadlock or a fatal crash, and automatically restarts the container. This self-healing mechanism ensures high availability by replacing unhealthy instances with fresh ones without requiring manual intervention from an operator or developer.
words.connector: Think of a liveness probe like a heartbeat monitor in a hospital — if the beat stops, an alarm sounds and the staff (Kubernetes) rushes in.
words.techContext: The liveness probe saved us last night by automatically restarting a pod that had frozen due to a memory leak.
words.exampleSentence: Be careful with liveness probes; if they are too aggressive, they might restart a healthy pod that is just temporarily under heavy load.

words.shortDefinition: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler; automatically scales the number of Pods in a deployment.
words.simpleDefinition: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler — a controller that automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas in a deployment based on CPU utilization or custom metrics. It ensures the application scales out to meet demand and in to save costs.
words.longDefinition: A built-in controller that automatically scales the number of pod replicas in a workload resource based on observed metrics like CPU or memory usage. By monitoring the resource consumption of existing pods, the HPA can increase the replica count to handle spikes in traffic or decrease it during low-usage periods to optimize resource costs. It can also be configured to use custom metrics, such as request latency or queue depth, for more specialized scaling.
words.connector: Think of HPA like an automatic door at a busy mall — it opens more doors (pods) when the crowd gets too large to keep moving.
words.techContext: We configured the HPA to maintain an average CPU usage of 70% across all pods in the web tier.
words.exampleSentence: The HPA automatically scaled our deployment from 2 to 10 pods within minutes of the marketing email being sent.

words.shortDefinition: Custom Resource Definition; a way to extend Kubernetes with your own API objects.
words.simpleDefinition: Custom Resource Definition — a mechanism allowing developers to extend the Kubernetes API with their own custom object types. These custom objects behave just like native resources, enabling the creation of specialized automation controllers and operators.
words.longDefinition: An extension mechanism that allows users to add their own custom objects to the Kubernetes API without needing to modify the core Kubernetes source code. Once a CRD is created, the custom resource can be managed using standard tools like kubectl and the Kubernetes API. When combined with a custom controller that monitors these resources, this enables the powerful 'Operator' pattern for automating the management of complex, stateful applications and infrastructure components.
words.connector: Think of CRDs like adding new, custom words to a dictionary — suddenly you can use them to describe things the language didn't originally support.
words.techContext: Most service meshes and monitoring tools use CRDs to allow you to configure them using standard 'kubectl' commands.
words.exampleSentence: We defined a CRD for our internal internal deployment pipeline to allow developers to request new environments using YAML.
